did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used machine learning (ANN, SVM, KNN, DT, NB, LR, RF, RS, Fuzzy Logic) and deep learning (CNN) algorithms to classify melanoma skin cancer dermoscopy datasets into benign and malignant lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the application and comparison of machine learning (ANN, SVM, KNN, DT, RF, NB, LR, RS, Fuzzy Logic) and deep learning (CNN) algorithms on two specific medical image datasets (chest X-ray and melanoma skin cancer). It includes detailed methodology, preprocessing, feature extraction, ablation tests, and performance results (accuracy, sensitivity, etc.), which are characteristic of an original research study rather than a literature review.

title: Performance Investigation for Medical Image Evaluation and Diagnosis Using Machine-Learning and Deep-Learning Techniques
brief_summary: This study investigates the performance of various machine learning and deep learning algorithms for diagnosing lung diseases and melanoma skin cancer using medical image datasets. The researchers applied nine machine learning classifiers (including ANN, SVM, KNN, RF, and LR) and a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to two datasets: a chest X-ray dataset for lung classification and a dermoscopy dataset of 300 melanoma images for skin lesion classification. The evaluation focused on metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and AUC to compare the effectiveness of these methods. Results indicated that the CNN model achieved the highest accuracy for lung disease classification at 95.1%, while the Artificial Neural Network (ANN) performed best for melanoma classification with 96.6% accuracy. The findings demonstrate that both ML and DL techniques are effective for medical image diagnosis, with specific algorithms outperforming others depending on the disease type.

lesion_types_studied.lesion_type: Melanoma skin cancer (benign and malignant lesions)
lesion_types_studied.details: The study classified a dermoscopy dataset of 300 melanoma skin cancer images into benign (145 images) and malignant (155 images) categories. The malignant class included specific types: superficial spreading, nodular, lentigo, and acral malignant melanoma.
The study also classified chest X-ray images into normal and abnormal lung categories, though this is a pulmonary condition rather than a skin lesion.

all_task_types.target: Lung disease status (normal vs. abnormal) and melanoma skin cancer status (benign vs. malignant)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is to 'classify the lungs in the first medical dataset into normal and abnormal, and skin lesions in the second medical dataset into malignant and benign.' It employs machine learning algorithms (ANN, SVM, KNN, DT, RF, NB, LR, RS, Fuzzy Logic) and a deep learning CNN model for binary classification tasks. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, recall, F-measure, and AUC.

datasets_used.name: Chest X-ray Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset of chest X-ray images obtained from Kaggle [16], containing samples of normal and abnormal lung cases. Images were captured in JPG format with various resolutions, standardized to 256 × 256 pixels.

use.training: Used for training machine learning (ANN, SVM, KNN, DT, RF, NB, LR, RS, Fuzzy Logic) and deep learning (CNN) models. Divided into 70% for training.
use.testing: Used for testing the classification models. Divided into 30% for testing.

datasets_used.name: Dermoscopy Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset of dermoscopy images of melanoma skin cancer obtained from The Lloyd Dermatology and Laser Center [17] and the Dermatology Online Atlas [18]. Images were captured in JPG format and standardized to 256 × 256 pixels.

use.training: Used for training machine learning (ANN, SVM, KNN, DT, RF, NB, LR, RS, Fuzzy Logic) and deep learning (CNN) models. Divided into 70% for training.
use.testing: Used for testing the classification models. Divided into 30% for testing.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between algorithms were based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, etc.) without statistical significance testing.

Chest X-ray Dataset (Lung Disease Classi.description: Classification of lung images into normal and abnormal categories using 10 algorithms (9 ML, 1 DL). Results reported in Table 10 and Table 12.

ResNet18.Note: Accuracy reported in Table 8 and Figure 19. Other metrics not explicitly listed in table format for this specific model in the provided text.

Proposed CNN (with extracted features).Note: Accuracy when inputs of extracted features for other ML methods were concatenated to the final layer.

Chest X-ray Dataset (Lung Disease Classi.notes: CNN provided the best outcomes for deep learning. RF performed best among ML techniques. DT attained the lowest performance (74%). LR, ANN, KNN, SVM, and RS were also effective.

Melanoma Skin Cancer Dermoscopy Dataset.description: Classification of skin lesions into benign and malignant categories using 10 algorithms (9 ML, 1 DL). Results reported in Table 11 and Table 12.

ResNet18.Note: Accuracy reported in Table 8 and Figure 19. Other metrics not explicitly listed in table format for this specific model in the provided text.

Proposed CNN (with extracted features).Note: Accuracy when inputs of extracted features for other ML methods were concatenated to the final layer.

Melanoma Skin Cancer Dermoscopy Dataset.notes: ANN outperformed all other ML algorithms. KNN, RF, RS, Fuzzy Logic, and CNN were also effective. NB classifier attained the lowest performance (80%). CNN achieved high accuracy (93%) but was outperformed by ANN in overall accuracy metrics.

architecture_used.main_model: Proposed CNN (custom architecture) and ResNet18 (pre-trained backbone)
architecture_used.pretraining: ResNet18 was used as a pre-trained model; the proposed CNN was trained from scratch.
architecture_used.input_size: 256x256 pixels for the proposed CNN; 224x224x3 for ResNet18

proposed_cnn.structure: Three convolution layers, three max-pooling layers, three batch-normalization layers, and one fully connected layer
proposed_cnn.layers_evaluated: Up to 25 layers evaluated in ablation tests

resnet18.blocks: Four identical ConvNet layers, each made up of two residual blocks

ann.structure: Input layer, two hidden layers, two output layers
ann.inputs: 39 inputs for chest X-ray dataset; 16 inputs for melanoma dataset (derived from extracted features)

classification_or_output_head.proposed_cnn: Fully connected layer feeding into sequential layer for classification
classification_or_output_head.resnet18: FC layer feeding data to sequential layer

preprocessing_techniques: Chest X-ray dataset: Image cropping (manual, square form focusing on lung ROI), noise removal (median filtering), contrast enhancement (contrast adjustment and histogram equalization)

Melanoma dermoscopy dataset: Hair/artifact removal (conversion to grayscale, black top-hat morphological filter for detection, thresholding to create binary mask, region fill to replace hair pixels with neighbors), Otsu thresholding, binarization, image negation

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Images were standardized to 256 × 256 pixels for the proposed CNN and chest X-ray dataset; resized to 224 × 224 × 3 for the ResNet18 model.
Used for preprocessing augmentation with the AugStatic package to achieve the 224 × 224 × 3 input size for the ResNet18 model.

conclusions: The Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) achieved the highest accuracy (95.1%) for lung disease classification on the chest X-ray dataset, outperforming other machine learning algorithms such as Random Forest (93.3%) and Logistic Regression (92%).

The Artificial Neural Network (ANN) performed best for melanoma skin cancer classification on the dermoscopy dataset with 96.6% accuracy, outperforming the CNN (93.3%) and other machine learning classifiers like KNN (95.5%) and Random Forest (94.6%).
Among machine learning algorithms for lung disease classification, Random Forest (RF) was the most effective, while the Decision Tree (DT) classifier attained the lowest performance (74%).
For melanoma classification, the Naïve Bayes (NB) classifier attained the lowest performance (80%), whereas KNN, RF, Random Subspace (RS), Fuzzy Logic, and CNN were also effective.

Ablation tests indicated that a 3x3 kernel size in convolutional layers had a strong positive impact, achieving over 90% accuracy for both datasets, while increasing the number of layers beyond a certain point did not necessarily improve performance.
The proposed CNN structure was adopted for diagnosis because it achieved high accuracy and is computationally less costly and faster to train compared to ResNet18.

Using manually extracted features concatenated to the CNN's final layer resulted in lower performance (83.1% for lung, 87.6% for melanoma) compared to the CNN trained directly on images, indicating that the network using raw features is more robust and effective.
Both machine learning and deep learning techniques are effective for medical image diagnosis, with specific algorithms outperforming others depending on the disease type and dataset characteristics.
important_remarks: The melanoma dataset is small (300 images) and sourced from specific private centers (The Lloyd Dermatology and Laser Center, Dermatology Online Atlas), raising concerns about population bias and generalizability to broader clinical populations.
The chest X-ray dataset is sourced from Kaggle, which may introduce selection bias or lack of standardized acquisition protocols compared to multi-center clinical trials.

No external validation was performed; the study relies solely on a single 70/30 train-test split for both datasets, increasing the risk of overfitting and data leakage if patient data is not strictly partitioned.

The paper does not report statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) for performance comparisons between algorithms, limiting the robustness of claims regarding superior model performance.

The CNN architecture is custom-built with only three convolutional layers, which is unusually shallow compared to state-of-the-art deep learning models, potentially limiting feature extraction capability for complex medical images.

The study compares traditional machine learning algorithms (using hand-crafted features) with deep learning (CNN) on the same datasets, but the feature extraction methods for ML are not fully detailed in the provided context, making it difficult to assess if the comparison is fair regarding information loss.

Preprocessing steps for the melanoma dataset include hair removal using morphological filters, which may inadvertently remove or alter lesion features, introducing potential artifacts or bias in the training data.
The study claims computational efficiency for the proposed CNN over ResNet18, but does not provide quantitative metrics (e.g., FLOPs, inference time, parameter count) to substantiate this claim.

The validation set consists of only 100 new images, which is insufficient for robust generalization assessment, especially given the small size of the original test sets (183 for lung, 89 for melanoma).

The paper does not address class imbalance explicitly in the methodology, although the melanoma dataset has a slight imbalance (145 benign vs. 155 malignant) and the lung dataset has a moderate imbalance (288 normal vs. 324 abnormal).
